body:
### Describe your issue

Some tests take a lot of time to run. We should investigate if there is anything easy to speed up some of them.

Current CI test runtimes in GHA with Python 3.14 / Linux:

</details>

Remarks:

In general, `EndToEnd` tests tend to be fairly time consuming because they run multiple separate Python commands in sequence. In some cases, we might still be able to "do less", parallelise module builds, etc.
We might also be able to tune the build commands (`cython`/`cythonize`), which might benefit users as well. There is only so much we can do, though, when most of the time is already spent in the C compiler.

We use `cython.inline()` in some tests to do runtime compilation. This is [known](https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/7488) to be slow due to C compilation. We might be able to replace some occurrences with simpler parser-only calls to `CythonTest.fragment()` or otherwise try to do less to get the same result.

comment_data.body: How about running just subset of tests and allowing to trigger GH Actions with full suite from GH comment? E.g. see https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/81382

For me, this ticket is more about not needlessly wasting resources. There are certainly code changes for which running the whole test suite is (waaay) too broad, but since it's essentially unbearable for developers to run the whole suite locally these days (and many issues only appear in some Python versions on some operating system, usually Windows), having to decide to cancel a certain job seems much safer than relying on maintainers to set a GH-label or write a comment to trigger an extended build. It certainly wouldn't be the first time for me that I hit "merge" as soon as I'm comforted by the partial test results, and then get bitten by a failure in some legacy PyPy-3.9 or old-MSVC job. Better safe than sorry.

> For me, this ticket is more about not needlessly wasting resources.

Fair enough. I thought if there are not low hanging fruits in general with different approach to testing. For me it is 99% waste of running the whole suite for every push I do in GH because 99% of my work is not related to C code so it is low possibility that I break a specific version of python. For me the ideal workflow would be to have small subset of tests run automatically and after before merging I would just request full tests suite to be sure that everything is fine - just my 2 cents.

If we can tie down the set of tests based on changed files, as in, changes to `Buffer.c` only affects buffer and memoryview tests, then running only that subset of the tests would certainly reduce the load. But then, changes in `ExprNodes.py` would probably continue to require a full test run and are difficult to slice further. And it generally makes me feel uncomfortable to have only a subset of tests running because tests tend to break in corners where you don't expect it. Otherwise, I'd probably have included the specific test in my local testing while I was developing the change.

Thus, I'm not sure if it's worth the effort.

We already have a distinction in the GHA workflow triggers to avoid running unrelated workflows.

One thing we could do is to cascade our test jobs further. We could move the (quick) codestyle job before all other tests to catch obvious deficiencies. Then, we could trigger a cython-only run (without C compilation, as we do for the coverage analysis) to see if we broke anything up to the C code generation, and only then run the complete tests. It would delay the final test result but would fail earlier and run less jobs on bigger mistakes. Maybe we can find a way to sidestep this cascade if there are utility code changes (which almost always require C compiler runs).

OTOH, failures to generate the C code should probably be caught during local development, so I'm not sure how much we'd gain from a cython-only run.

> cascade our test jobs further.

Maybe the useful cascading would be 

* Code style?
* Ubuntu x64 recent py version
* Ubuntu x64 all py versions
* Everything else

In the sense that usual if something's broken then it's broken everywhere. Next most likely is that some specific Python version is broken. And it's pretty unusual to have a problem with windows/Mac but it does happen occasionally. 

Obviously that'd extend the wall-clock time to run though

> cascade our test jobs further.

I'm looking into this. I think we can afford to run the code style and quick single-Python cross-platform tests in the first go, then follow up with the multi-Python tests. The problem I often had was that GHA chose uninteresting jobs to start first, so enforcing cross-platform tests should already give a good idea how the push performs.

title: Investigate slow tests
